title: Dependency Failure Resilience Playbook (Enterprise Onboarding)
preview:
Purpose

This playbook documents a standardized approach for identifying, preventing, and remediating failures caused by third-party or internal dependency outages during enterprise onboarding and early go-live. It focuses on the technical mitigations, telemetry assertions, and communication patterns Customer Success (CS), SRE, and Platform teams should follow when dependencies degrade or fail.

Scope

- Applies to enterprise onboarding windows, canary rollouts, and first-72-hour post-go-live stability runs.
- Covers external partners (cloud provider APIs, SCM, identity providers, SaaS connectors) and internal services we rely on (ingress-gateway, fanout-proxy, telemetry-gatekeeper, embedding-indexer, kvcache, model-router).
- Does NOT cover purely customer-side outages (e.g., customer DB down) except where we coordinate cutover.

Owners and RACI

- Primary owner: Customer Success (Onboarding) — Ava Chen
- Technical owner (SRE escalation): Eng SRE — Diego Morales
- Platform mitigations and config changes: Eng Platform — Priya Natarajan
- Observability contract owner: Eng DevEx — Samir Gupta

RACI summary:
1) Detection: Eng SRE (R), Eng DevEx (C), CS (I)
2) Triage/Customer comms: CS (R), Eng SRE (A)
3) Mitigation (routing/fallback): Eng Platform (R/A)
4) Postmortem & action items: Eng SRE (R), CS (C)

Pre-Go-Live Dependency Map Checklist (must be completed before go-live gate)

1. Dependency inventory: maintain a single table with service name, owner, SLA (external), API endpoints, auth method, and last verified test run.
2. Telemetry assertions: add at least three assertion checks for each dependency (latency, error rate, presence of expected metrics).
3. Fallback compatibility: confirm an alternate path exists for each critical dependency (e.g., cached read, degraded feature, queued processing).
4. Runbook verification: ensure a runbook paragraph exists for each item with detection signal and mitigation steps.
5. Communication template: verify customer comms and internal incident templates are available and signed-off.

Dependency Resilience Matrix (example table)

| Dependency | Criticality | SLO tie | Failure modes | Primary detection signal | Immediate mitigation | Owner |
|-----------|-------------|---------|---------------|--------------------------|----------------------|-------|
| ingress-gateway | P0 | 99.9% availability | TLS handshake failures, 502 spikes | 5xx rate > 0.5% over 5m, TLS errors spike | Reroute to standby gateway, enable traffic-shaping to slow new sessions | Eng Platform (Priya) |
| telemetry-gatekeeper | P0 | Inference telemetry 1h TTL | Missing spans, dropped metrics | Missing ingestion heartbeat for 2m, sample rate drop | Fallback to passive buffering sidecar; enable synchronous flush | Eng DevEx (Samir) |
| fanout-proxy | P1 | 99% throughput | Queue growth, per-tenant blocking | Queue depth > 80% capacity for 3m | Enable per-tenant throttles, route low-priority tenants to degraded path | Eng Platform |
| external-idp (SCIM) | P2 | External SLA 99.5% | 429/403 from idp | auth failures or 429s > 1% | Switch to cached user mapping; pause provisioning jobs | Customer Success |
| embedding-indexer (internal) | P1 | Indexing SLA 95% | Index lag, OOM | indexing-lag > 10m | Apply incremental backfill window, drop non-critical batches | Eng Serving Runtime |
| model-router | P0 | Serving SLA 99.95% | Model load failures, KV cache misses | model load errors, cache miss rate > 15% | Switch to compatible quantized variant, enable cache warmup | Eng Serving Runtime |

Operational Runbook (detection -> mitigation -> validate)

1. Detection (first 5 minutes)
   - Watchers: SRE alerting channels receive automated signals (PagerDuty, Slack #sre-ops).
   - Initial checks: run ./tools/check-dependency-health --service <name> to confirm alarm.

2. Triage (5–20 minutes)
   - Assign a triage lead (SRE) and a customer liaison (CS).
   - Confirm blast radius: run tenant impact query (example):

     curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $MONITOR_API" "https://telemetry-api.prod/tenant-impact?service=fanout-proxy&window=10m" | jq .

   - If impact > threshold (defined below) escalate to on-call Eng Platform.

3. Immediate mitigations (20–60 minutes)
   - If ingress-gateway is degraded: flip to standby gateway with the CLI:

     rwdctl gateway failover --from ingress-gw-us-west-2 --to ingress-gw-us-east-1 --reason "onboarding surge"

   - If telemetry-gatekeeper ingestion is missing: enable buffering and start a fast-backfill:

     kubectl -n telemetry apply -f buffering-sidecar-override.yaml
     ./tools/backfill-telemetry --start $(date -u -d '15 minutes ago' +%s) --end $(date -u +%s)

   - If model-router fails to load a quantized variant: re-route traffic to fallback model:

     rwdctl model route --from model:prod:v2 --to model:prod:quantized-v1 --percent 100 --force

4. Validate (after 60 minutes)
   - Confirm telemetry asserts: all dependency assertions pass for 10 consecutive minutes.
   - Confirm SLO posture re-evaluated for impacted tenants and document customer-facing impact.

Escalation thresholds (examples)

- Severity P0: Any customer-facing error rate causing >1% 5xx across the tenant fleet or single large tenant with >0.5% 5xx for 10m. PagerDuty P0, incident bridge, executive notify.
- Severity P1: Throughput drops causing queue growth beyond 50% for >15m. PagerDuty P1; CS alerts impacted customers.
- Severity P2: Auxiliary systems (SCIM, billing connector) errors causing background job failures. Email + Slack notify; CS to log and monitor.

Customer Communication templates

- Initial acknowledgement (under 15 minutes):
  "We’ve detected a degradation affecting [feature/service]. Our engineering team is triaging. We will provide an update within 30 minutes and a root-cause timeline within 4 hours.

  Impact: [brief impact].
  Mitigation: [what we’re doing].
  Contact: your CS rep, [name], + link to status page."

- Mitigation update (30–90 minutes):
  "Summary: We identified [dependency] as the primary cause. We’ve applied [mitigation] which restored service for X% of requests. Engineers continue to monitor and will follow up with next steps by [time]."

Telemetry Assertions (examples to add to onboarding checks)

1. Ingestion heartbeat: telemetry-gatekeeper.heartbeat == true in last 1m
2. Model load success rate: model-router.load_success_rate >= 99.9% over 5m
3. KV cache hit ratio: kvcache.hit_ratio >= 85% over 10m

Add these to the onboarding synthetic suite; example JSON snippet for assertion: 

{
  "assertion_name": "kvcache_hit_ratio_onboarding",
  "expr": "avg(kvcache_hit_ratio{env=\"prod\",stage=\"onboarding\"}) > 0.85",
  "window": "10m",
  "owner": "eng-serving-runtime"
}

Canary Failure Simulation (run prior to go-live)

1. Simulate partial dependency failure: throttle upstream responses to 50% for 10 minutes using traffic-shaper job.
2. Verify that fallback paths engage within target time (<= 30s) and degraded responses are metric-tagged.
3. Run customer communication dry-run: ask CS to publish the initial acknowledgement and verify internal observers see it.

Post-Incident Walkthrough and Action Items (within 72h)

- Convene triage + CS + platform; capture timeline and mitigation chronology.
- Generate action items: code fix, observability gaps, config changes, customer compensation if SLA breach. Assign owners and target dates.
- Update dependency map and onboarding checklist with any missed validations.

Common Failure Mode Playbooks (short form)

- Telemetry ingestion drop
  1) Buffer sidecar enablement
  2) Backfill telemetry for the gap window
  3) Postmortem: add assertion and test to synthetic suite

- External SCIM throttling
  1) Pause provisioning
  2) Switch to cached identity mapping for auth flows
  3) Retry queue with exponential backoff and jitter

- KV cache cold start leading to latency spike
  1) Warm cache using sample keyset: ./tools/warm-kvcache --tenant <tenant-id> --keys-file warm-keys.json
  2) If warmfail, route to model variant with smaller memory footprint

Appendix: Useful commands and references

- rwdctl status dependencies --tenant <id> --since 15m\- shows dependency health summary\- 
- ./tools/check-dependency-health --service telemetry-gatekeeper --verbose\- detailed probe for the service\-
- Example buffering-sidecar-override.yaml (snippet):

apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: telemetry-buffer-override
data:
  buffer.max_bytes: "50000000"
  flush.interval_s: "10"

Appendix: Postmortem checklist template (to attach to incident)

- Incident title and severity
- Detection time, mitigation applied, time to recovery
- Impacted tenants (list) and customer-facing communication timeline
- Root cause and contributing factors
- Concrete action items (owner, due date, verification test)

FAQ (short)

Q: When should CS ask for credits or SLA remedies?
A: Follow the existing SLA reconciliation playbook — if the incident breaches the contracted uptime or per-tenant SLO defined in the contract, CS should initiate the billing remediation flow after the postmortem is complete.

Q: Can we always failover to another region?
A: Not always. Region-based failover is a last-resort for data residency or private deployment customers. Follow the regional failover checklist and confirm customer consent where required.

Related pages and references

- Onboarding failure mode scenarios (linked internally)
- Canary simulation playbook (Eng SRE)
- SLA remediation and billing process

This playbook is intended to be a living document. Update the dependency matrix and telemetry assertions after every simulation and postmortem.
